at the waterhole.] [Three different herds came together to drink.] [This is an unusual sight, as elephants usually travel in smaller groups.] [The first herd consists of 12 elephants.] [This is the smallest of the three herds that gathered today.] [The second herd is twice as large as the first herd.] [This means the second herd has double the number of elephants in the first herd.] [To calculate this, we multiply the number of elephants in the first herd by two.] [12 multiplied by two equals 24.] [So, the second herd has 24 elephants.] [The third herd is 5 elephants smaller than the second herd.] [This means we subtract 5 from the number of elephants in the second herd.] [24 minus 5 equals 19.] [Therefore, the third herd has 19 elephants.] [Now, we need to find the total number of elephants at the waterhole.] [We do this by adding the number of elephants in all three herds.] [The first herd has 12 elephants.] [The second herd has 24 elephants.] [The third herd has 19 elephants.] [Adding these together gives us 12 plus 24 plus 19.] [12 plus 24 equals 36.] [36 plus 19 equals 55.] [So, the total number of elephants at the waterhole is 55.] [This is a significant gathering of elephants.] [It shows that the waterhole is an important resource for these animals.] [The indigenous community member is pleased to see this.] [They know that elephants rely on water sources for survival.] [This event may indicate a change in the environment.] [The herds may have been searching for water due to dry conditions.] [The indigenous member has observed similar patterns before.] [They have learned to read the signs of nature.] [The elephants’ behavior is a reflection of the ecosystem.] [Tracking their movements helps in understanding the land.] [The waterhole is a
